content: Just as adaptive memory retrieval in the human brain relies on dynamic prefrontal-hippocampal interaction to prioritize relevant information and work around capacity limits, state-of-the-art active learning AI agents can be redesigned to use a analogous two-component interaction system: a "prefrontal" controller that gates retrieval cues based on current task goals, and a "hippocampal" episodic memory store that selectively reactivates past experiences to reduce working memory overload during sequential planning. This biologically inspired interaction mechanism addresses the longstanding problem of capacity limits in agent reasoning during long-horizon active learning tasks.
